questions.text: Platyhelminthes means:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Platyhelminthes is a phylum of invertebrates that includes flatworms. "Platy" means flat, and "helminths" refers to worms. Therefore, "Platyhelminthes" directly translates to "flatworms."
Roundworms belong to the phylum Nematoda, which is a distinct group from Platyhelminthes. Roundworms have a cylindrical, round body, unlike the flat body of flatworms.
Segmented worms belong to the phylum Annelida, characterized by their body divisions into segments, unlike the largely unsegmented flatworms.
The term "Platyhelminthes" does have a specific meaning, referring to a group of flatworms, so this isn't the correct option.

questions.explanation: Word Platyhelminthes (derived from the Greek platy, meaning "flat" while helminth meaning "worm") are a phylum of relatively simple bilaterian, unsegmented, soft-bodied invertebrates.

questions.text: Which of them is not poikilotherm?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Spiny anteaters(Echidnas) are mammals and, like most mammals, are endothermic, meaning they can regulate their body temperature internally. They are not poikilothermic (cold-blooded).
Lizards are ectothermic, or poikilothermic, meaning their body temperature fluctuates with the surrounding environment.
Crocodiles are also ectothermic, or poikilothermic, relying on external sources to regulate their body temperature.
Snakes are ectothermic animals, depending on external sources to control their body temperature. They are poikilothermic.

questions.explanation: A poikilotherm is an animal whose internal temperature varies considerably. It is the opposite of a homeotherm, an animal which maintains thennal homeostasis. Spiny ant eaters belong to homeotherm.

questions.text: Opossum and koala bear belongs to the sub-class:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This subclass includes the egg-laying mammals, such as the platypus and echidna.
Eutherian mammals are placental mammals, which give birth to relatively developed young ones.
This subclass comprises marsupial mammals, like opossums and koalas, which give birth to relatively undeveloped young that further develop in a pouch.
Monotremes are egg-laying mammals, such as the platypus and echidna.

questions.explanation: Metatheria includes marsupials that possess a pouch and give birth to partially-developed young ones. Well-known marsupials include kangaroos, wallabies, koalas, opossums, woinbats, Tasmanian devils, and the extinct Thylacme.

questions.text: The central nervous system of chordates is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The term "ventral" refers to the underside or belly side of an organism. In chordates, the central nervous system is not located ventrally. Instead, it is typically positioned dorsally, along the back.

This option suggests that the central nervous system is both ventral and hollow. While the neural tube, which gives rise to the CNS, begins as a hollow structure in the early embryonic stages, the mature CNS is not ventrally positioned. It develops dorsally.
The lateral sides of an organism are the sides. The central nervous system is not typically located laterally; instead, it is positioned along the dorsal (back) side.

This option is correct. The central nervous system of chordates is dorsal (along the back) and initially develops from a hollow structure called the neural tube. The neural tube gives rise to the brain and spinal cord.

questions.explanation: The central nervous system of all chordates is dorsal and hollow.The central nervous system of chordates is dorsal (along the back) and initially develops from a hollow structure called the neural tube. The neural tube gives rise to the brain and spinal cord.

questions.text: All are characteristics of mollusks except:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Mollusks are known for having unsegmented, soft bodies, often protected by a hard shell.
Mollusks develop from three embryonic layers, making them triploblastic organisms.
Mollusks exhibit bilateral symmetry, meaning their body can be divided into symmetrical halves on a single plane.
Mollusks are not pseudocoelomates; they are true coelomates, with a fully lined body cavity.

questions.explanation: Mollusks are characterized by being soft-bodied, triploblastic, and exhibiting bilateral symmetry. They are true coelomates, meaning they have a true body cavity lined with mesoderm. Therefore, the statement that they are 'pseudocoelomates' is incorrect, as pseudocoelomates have a body cavity that is not fully lined by mesoderm. The other options correctly describe characteristics of mollusks.

questions.text: In animals, a reproductive system developed from:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The ectoderm is one of the three germ layers in the early embryo. It gives rise to structures such as the skin, nervous system, and epidermal tissues. While the nervous system plays a role in reproductive processes, the reproductive system itself does not develop directly from the ectoderm.

The blastoderm is not a specific germ layer but rather a stage in early embryonic development. During blastoderm formation, the embryo is divided into layers, including the three germ layers (ectoderm, mesoderm, and endoderm). The reproductive system develops from one of these germ layers.
Mesoderm gives rise to muscular, skeletal, and reproductive systems.

The endoderm is another of the three germ layers, and it contributes to the development of internal organs such as the digestive tract. While the endoderm indirectly influences the reproductive system through its role in supporting overall organismal development, the reproductive organs themselves primarily originate from the mesoderm.

questions.explanation: Mesoderm gives rise to muscular, skeletal, and reproductive systems.

questions.text: During embryonic development the mouth is formed at some distance anterior to the blastopore and the blastopore forms the anus in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: When blastopore develops into anus then these animals are called deuterostomates. Echinoderms and chordates are deuterostomates.
Molluscs are protostomates , in them blastopore develops into mouth .
Nematods are pseudocoelomates therefore they are not grouped into protostomes and deuterostomes.
These are also pseudocoelomates.

questions.explanation: A. When blastopore develops into anus then these animals are called deuterostomates. Echinoderms and chordates are deuterostomates.B. Molluscs are protostomates , in them blastopore develops into mouth .C. Nematods are pseudocoelomates therefore they are not grouped into protostomes and deuterostomes.D. These are also pseudocoelomates.

questions.text: Which of the following does not have specialized respiratory organs?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Hydra belong to the phylum Cnidarians.They have no respiratory, excretory and circulatory system.

Cockroaches belong to the class Insecta and possess a well-developed respiratory system adapted specifically for terrestrial life. Instead of lungs, they breathe through a network of tiny tubes called tracheae, which open to the outside through small pores known as spiracles located along the sides of their body. Air enters through the spiracles and travels directly to the tissues through the branching tracheal system, allowing efficient gas exchange without the need for blood to transport oxygen. This proper and specialized respiratory system enables cockroaches to survive in a wide range of environments.

Birds have well-developed and highly efficient respiratory organs that enable them to meet the high oxygen demand required for flight. Their respiratory system includes lungs and a unique network of air sacs that allow continuous airflow through the lungs, ensuring that fresh air is available during both inhalation and exhalation. This system provides a constant supply of oxygen, making bird respiration far more efficient than that of most other vertebrates. Because of these specialized structures, birds maintain high metabolic activity and sustain long periods of flight.
Hydras donot have but cockroaches do have respiratory system.

questions.explanation: Hydra has neither blood and blood vessels, nor organs of excretion and respiration, which are carried on individually by each cell. Due to the thinness of the body wall and circulation of water in the gastrovascular cavity, most of the cells of the body remain freely exposed to the surrounding water.

questions.text: Tse-tse fly causes the sleeping sickness and skin diseases by transmitting:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Plasmodium is the protozoan parasite that causes malaria, a mosquito-borne disease. It is not transmitted by the tse-tse fly.
Tse-tse fly causes the sleeping sickness and skin diseases by transmitting Trypanosoma
Anopheles is a genus of mosquito that is known to transmit the Plasmodium parasite that causes malaria. It is not directly involved in the transmission of sleeping sickness.
This option is also incorrect because insects can transmit a wide range of diseases, but not sleeping sickness.

questions.explanation: Explanation:Tse-tse fly causes the sleeping sickness and skin diseases by transmitting Trypanosoma. Plasmodium is the protozoan parasite that causes malaria, a mosquito-borne disease. It is not transmitted by the tse-tse fly. Anopheles is a genus of mosquito that is known to transmit the Plasmodium parasite that causes malaria. It is not directly involved in the transmission of sleeping sickness. This option is also incorrect because insects can transmit a wide range of diseases, but not sleeping sickness.

questions.text: A parasite living inside body of the host is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Ectoparasite;parasite that lives outside of its host
obligate parasite is the parasite that cannot complete its life cycle without exploiting a host
Facultative Parasite’s life cycle does not completely depend on the host
Endoparasite e.g tapeworm that lives insides the host

questions.explanation: [A] Ectoparasite;parasite that lives outside of its host[B] obligate parasite is the parasite that cannot complete its life cycle without exploiting a host[C] Facultative Parasite’s life cycle does not completely depend on the host[D] Endoparasite e.g tapeworm that lives insides the host

questions.text: Trypanosoma is transmitted in human beings by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Plasmodium is the parasite responsible for causing malaria, and it is transmitted to humans through the bite of infected female Anopheles mosquitoes. It is not associated with Trypanosoma transmission.
House flies are not known to transmit Trypanosoma to humans. They are involved in the transmission of various other diseases, but not Trypanosoma.
Anopheles mosquitoes transmit Plasmodium, not Trypanosoma. They are responsible for the spread of malaria.

This is the correct answer. The tsetse fly is a vector for Trypanosoma parasites that cause African trypanosomiasis, also known as sleeping sickness. When an infected tsetse fly bites a human, it can transmit the Trypanosoma parasites, leading to the disease.

questions.explanation: Trypanosoma, the cause of sleeping sickness and skin diseases. The common house ly carries disease causing organisms to contaminate food and cause cholera, hepatitis etc. Some species of Trypanosoma cause diseases in cattle, also.

questions.text: In case of poriferans, the spongocoel is lined with flagellated cells called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A large main opening of poriferans is called osculum.
Spongocoel is the central body cavity of the sponges. It is lined by highly specialised flagellated cells called choanocytes.
The middle region between pinachoderm and choanoderm in sponges is called mesenchyme.
Ostia are the perforated pores in sponges.

questions.explanation: Spongocoel is the central body cavity of the sponges. It is lined by highly specialised flagellated cells called choanocytes.

questions.text: Which of the following are correctly matched with respect to their taxonomic classification?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Housefly, butterfly, Insecta tsetse fly, silverfish
options.explanation: Yes, houseflies, butterflies, tsetse flies, and silverfish all belong to the same phylum in the animal kingdom, which is Arthropoda. The phylum Arthropoda is characterized by having jointed legs and a segmented exoskeleton, and it includes a vast diversity of invertebrate animals, including insects (like houseflies, butterflies, and tsetse flies) and other arthropods (like silverfish).

options.text: Spiny anteater, sea urchin, sea cucumber
options.explanation: Spiny ant eater (Echidna) is a prototherian mammal whereas, sea urchins and sea cucumbers are echinoderms.

options.text: Flying fish, Cuttle fish, Pisces silver fish
options.explanation: Silver fish (Lepisma) is an insect, Cuttle fish (Sepia) is a mollusc and flying fish (Exocoetus) is a bony fish.
Centipede is class chilopoda, Millipede is class diplopoda and Scorpion and Spider are class arachnida of Phylum Arthropoda.

questions.explanation: Houseflies, butterflies, tsetse flies, and silverfish all belong to the same phylum in the animal kingdom, which is Arthropoda. Spiny ant eater (Echidna) is a prototherian mammal whereas, sea urchins and sea cucumbers are echinoderms. Silver fish (Lepisma) is an insect, Cuttle fish (Sepia) is a mollusc and flying fish (Exocoetus) is a bony fish. Centipede is class chilopoda, Millipede is class diplopoda and Scorpion and Spider are class arachnida of Phylum Arthropoda.

questions.text: In which of the following animals is the brain absent but nerve cells are present?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Sponges are the only multicellular animals without a nervous system. They do not have any nerve cells or sensory cells.
The nervous system of an earthworm comprises of a ventral nerve cord and peripheral nerves.
The cockroach's nervous system is composed of a series of fused, segmentally organised ganglia connected by paired longitudinal connectives on the ventral side.

Hydra belongs to the phylum Coelenterata and has nerve cells but no brain. Its nervous system consists of nerve cells and their processes, and sensory cells are also present. Sponges do not have nerve cells because they lack a nervous system. The earthworm (Annelida) has a nervous system consisting of a circumenteric nerve ring and a solid, double, mid-ventral nerve cord with ganglia. The cockroach (Arthropoda) has a nervous system similar to that of the earthworm.

questions.explanation: Hydra belongs to the phylum Coelenterata and has nerve cells but no brain. Its nervous system consists of nerve cells and their processes, and sensory cells are also present. Sponges do not have nerve cells because they lack a nervous system. The earthworm (Annelida) has a nervous system consisting of a circumenteric nerve ring and a solid, double, mid-ventral nerve cord with ganglia. The cockroach (Arthropoda) has a nervous system similar to that of the earthworm.

questions.text: Pseudocoelom develops from:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A blastostyle is a structure found in some invertebrates, particularly hydrozoans, which are part of the phylum Cnidaria. The blastostyle gives rise to medusae, which are jellyfish-like individuals in the life cycle of hydrozoans. Medusae, in turn, release gametes for sexual reproduction, leading to the formation of new colonies through a combination of sexual and asexual reproduction in the hydrozoan life cycle.
The body cavity that is formed from Blastocoel (cavity that is formed at blastula stage of developing embryo) is called pseudocoelom.
This is incorrect.A blastocyst is an early stage of embryo development in mammals (including humans).It forms about 5–6 days after fertilization and is the stage that implants into the uterus.
Mesoderm gives rise to reproductive system.

questions.explanation: The body cavity that is formed from Blastocoel (cavity that is formed at blastula stage of developing embryo) is called pseudocoelom.

questions.text: A body cavity is absent in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Acoelomates lack a body cavity, or coelom, between the body wall and the digestive system. This group includes the phylum Platyhelminthes, known as flatworms.
Pseudocoelomates possess a pseudocoelom, a body cavity that is not fully lined with mesoderm. Examples include the phylum Nematoda (roundworms).
This option is incorrect because pseudocoelomates do possess a type of body cavity, although it is not a true coelom.
Coelomates possess a true coelom, a body cavity fully lined with mesoderm. This group includes most animals, such as annelids, mollusks, and vertebrates.

questions.explanation: Acoelomates lack a body cavity, or coelom, between the body wall and the digestive system. This group includes the phylum Platyhelminthes, known as flatworms.

questions.text: In acoelomates, gut is lined externally by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The visceral mesoderm is a layer of the mesoderm in embryonic development that surrounds and supports the internal organs or viscera, including the heart, lungs, and digestive organs. It plays a vital role in the formation and development of these organs during embryogenesis, providing structural and functional support to ensure proper organ function.

The parietal mesoderm is another layer of the mesoderm that forms during early embryonic development. It lines the body cavity walls and is involved in creating the connective tissues, bones, and muscles of the limbs and trunk, contributing to the overall body structure.

The splanchnic mesoderm is a layer of the mesoderm that develops during embryogenesis. It surrounds the endoderm-derived organs, such as the heart, blood vessels, and portions of the digestive and respiratory systems, playing a crucial role in their formation and support.

The endoderm is one of the three primary germ layers in early embryonic development, giving rise to the innermost tissue layer in animals. It forms the lining of the digestive tract and respiratory system and contributes to various internal organs like the liver, pancreas, and lungs.

questions.explanation: The visceral mesoderm is a layer of the mesoderm in embryonic development that surrounds and supports the internal organs or viscera, including the heart, lungs, and digestive organs. It plays a vital role in the formation and development of these organs during embryogenesis, providing structural and functional support to ensure proper organ function.

questions.text: Which system is more developed in acoelomates?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Acoelomate has sac type digestive system, no respiratory and osmoregulatory systems. They have tubular excretory system called protonephridia having flame cells.
Acoelomates lack respiratory organs.Gas exchange occurs directly through the body surface by diffusion, so respiratory system is not developed.
Many acoelomates (like Planaria) have incomplete or branched gut, no anus.Digestive system is less developed compared to excretory system.
The excretory system (protonephridia) also performs osmoregulation, but the main system is the excretory system.So osmoregulation is not a separate system, it’s a function of the excretory system.

questions.explanation: Acoelomate has sac type digestive system, no respiratory and osmoregulatory systems. They have tubular excretory system called protonephridia having flame cells.

questions.text: Fate of the blastomeres is foretold in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Echinoderms exhibit radial cleavage, but the "fate" of blastomeres is not specifically foretold in this manner.
These organisms share some embryonic features with chordates but don't typically have a fate foretold through blastomeres in the same sense.
Arthropods show a spiral and determination cleavage and there fate of each blastomere is foretold.
Chordates, including vertebrates, exhibit various embryonic development patterns, but the fate of blastomeres in the same predictive manner as in arthropods isn't a defining characteristic.

questions.explanation: Arthropods are proterostomes in which cleavage is determinate (fate of blastomeres is foretold). Each blastomere developed into its special structure.

questions.text: _ is a triploblastic organism.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Jellyfish: This option is not the correct answer. Jellyfish belong to phylum Cnidaria. They are diploblastic and contain only two germ layers i.e, ectoderm and endoderm.
Sea Anemone: This option is not correct. Sea anemones belong to phylum Cnidaria. They are diploblastic and contain only two germ layers i.e, ectoderm and endoderm.

Tapeworm: This option is the correct answer. Tapeworms are triploblastic organisms. They belong to the phylum Platyhelminthes and have three germ layers: ectoderm, mesoderm and endoderm. They are considered triploblastic
Corals: This option is not the correct answer. Corals belong to phylum Cnidaria. They are diploblastic and contain only two germ layers i.e, ectoderm and endoderm.

questions.explanation: Tapeworms are triploblastic organisms. They belong to the phylum Platyhelminthes and have three germ layers: ectoderm, mesoderm and endoderm. They are considered triploblastic.
